revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:延喜式神名帳]][[simple:Engishiki Jinmyōchō]][[zh:延喜式神名帳]]
[[ja:延喜式神名帳]] [[File:Engishiki Jingi cropped Engishiki Vol 2 Flame 37.jpg|thumb|300px|Text of volume 9 of the ''Engishiki'', as included in the Japanese Classics Complete Works<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本古典全集 延喜式 第二|year=1929|publisher=日本古典全集刊行會|page=65|editor=[[正宗敦夫]]|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1912589/1/36}}</ref>]] '''Engishiki Jinmyōchō''' (延喜式神名帳) is the collective title for volumes 9 and 10 (Jinmyō-shiki upper and lower) of the ''Engishiki'', compiled in Enchō 5 (927)<ref>{{ill|Enchō|en|Enchō|qid=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}}5</ref><ref name=":0">{{Kotobank|神名帳}}</ref>.
== Nature and Establishment of the Jinmyōchō == The term ''Jinmyōchō'' originally referred to the official registry of Imperial shrines created by the Department of Divinities under the ancient Ritsuryō system. It listed shrines by province and district, indicating their offerings rank and number of seats, but did not include enshrined kami names or histories. The Engishiki Jinmyōchō reproduces the registry as it existed at the time of the Engishiki's compilation.
=== Shikinaisha === Shrines recorded in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō, and their modern identified successors (ronsha), are known as ''Shikinaisha'' (式内社) or simply ''Shikisha'' (式社). There were 2,861 such shrines nationwide, enshrining 3,132 kami<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=延喜式神名帳 上|year=1939|publisher=古典保存会|author=[[藤原時平]]等奉勅撰|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/3438688/1/5}}</ref>. By the early 10th century, these were officially recognized by the court as state shrines, reflecting political considerations in their selection.
=== Shikigaisha === Shrines existing at the time but not listed in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō are called ''Shikigaisha'' (式外社). These include shrines outside court control, powerful regional shrines such as Kumano Nachi Taisha, shrines that became temples under shinbutsu-shūgō, and those managed by Buddhist clergy like Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine.
=== Shrines Recorded in National Histories === Shrines not listed in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō but appearing in the Six National Histories are termed ''Kokushi Kenzai-sha'' (国史見在社) or simply ''Kenzai-sha''. These include Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine and Ōharano Shrine<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=神典解説 下巻|year=1939|publisher=大倉精神文化研究所|page=207|editor=[[大倉精神文化研究所]]|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1920679/1/111}}</ref>.
== Shrine Ranks in the Shikinaisha == [[File:Engishiki cropped Engishiki Vol 2 Flame 67.jpg|thumb|200px|Excerpt for ''Tōtōmi Province'' from volume 9 of the ''Engishiki'' (Japanese Classics Complete Works)<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本古典全集 延喜式 第二|year=1929|publisher=日本古典全集刊行會|page=65|editor=[[正宗敦夫]]|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1912589/1/36}}</ref>]] [[File:Engishiki cropped Engishiki Vol 2 Flame 69.jpg|thumb|200px|Excerpt for ''Suruga Province'' from volume 9 of the ''Engishiki''<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=日本古典全集 延喜式 第二|year=1929|publisher=日本古典全集刊行會|page=129|editor=正宗敦夫|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1912589/1/68}}</ref>]] Shikinaisha are categorized by the source of offerings and size into Kanpeitaisha (官幣大社), Kokpeitaisha (国幣大社), Kanpeishōsha (官幣小社), and Kokpeishōsha (国幣小社). 198 shrines (304 seats) were Kanpeitaisha, 155 shrines (188 seats) Kokpeitaisha, 375 shrines (433 seats) Kanpeishōsha, and 2,133 shrines (2,207 seats) Kokpeishōsha. Kanpeitaisha were concentrated in the Kinai region, while provincial shrines lay outside.
{| class="wikitable" ! Kanpeitaisha | 198 shrines || 304 positions |- ! Kokpeitaisha | 155 shrines || 188 positions |- ! Kanpeishōsha | 375 shrines || 433 positions |- ! Kokpeishōsha | 2,133 shrines || 2,207 positions |}
Some Shikinaisha also received offerings at festivals beyond the Kinensai, namely the Myōjin-sai (名神祭), Tsukinami-sai (月次祭), Akiname-sai (相嘗祭), and Niiname-sai (新嘗祭).
== Ronsha == Research to identify which modern shrines descend from Shikinaisha has long been conducted. Such modern successors, whether verified or presumed by tradition, are called ''Ronsha'' or ''Hiteisha''. Due to changes in names, deities, locations, mergers, and restorations, multiple shrines may claim descent from a single Shikinaisha.
== Footnotes ==
=== Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku'', Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai ed., Kōgakkan University Press, 1977–86 * ''Shikinaisha no Kenkyū'', Tsuyoshi Shiga, Yuzankaku, 1977–87 * {{Cite book|和書|author=Takao Nishimuta|title=延喜式神名帳の研究|origdate=1996-08-08|publisher=国書刊行会|isbn=9784336038081}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|de|Myōjin-Taisha|ja|名神大社|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|qid=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}} * {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qid=Q712534|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|qid=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * {{ill|Chokusaisha|en|Chokusaisha|qid=Q175288|lt=Chokusaisha|1=Chokusaisha}}
== External Links == * {{ill|National Diet Library Digital Collections|de|Digitale Sammlung der Nationalen Diätbibliothek|ja|国立国会図書館のデジタル化資料|zh|国立国会図书馆数字收藏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Diet Library Digital Collections|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: ''Engishiki'' vol. 2 (frames 37 onward)
